introSection: Cyclone is a wooden roller coaster located at Luna Park in Coney Island, Brooklyn, New York.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
In spring 1972, it was announced that Cyclone would be removed to make way for expansion of a nearby aquarium. Following the Save The Cyclone campaign, this did not happen and the coaster reopened on July 3, 1975.

The ride was declared a city landmark in 1988 and a national landmark in 1991. The Brooklyn Cyclones baseball team is named after the Cyclone.

The ride was awarded ACE Roller Coaster Landmark status by American Coaster Enthusiasts on June 29, 2002.

The ride underwent refurbishment work by Great Coasters International and Skyline Attractions in the late 2010s.

sections.title: Design
sections.content:
The Cyclone's compact layout has been duplicated on many other roller coasters.

The Cyclone does not use a ride computer. Braking ability is provided by manually operated skid brakes. The ride mechanics are mostly unchanged from the ride's opening.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: The ride is only designed to run one train at a time.

sections.title: Incidents
sections.content:
* On May 26, 1985, a 29-year-old male stood up and was killed after striking his head on a part of the structure.
 * On August 23, 1988, a maintenance worker was killed after assuming an unauthorized riding position and being ejected from the car. He fell 30 feet onto another section of track.
 * On July 31, 2007, a 53-year-old man suffered several crushed vertebrae in his neck while on the ride. He died to to complications in surgery 4 days later.
 * A woman claimed to be seriously injured after riding the Cyclone in 2008. Despite being partially at fault for the incident, she was awarded $1.5 million in damages.

fullText:
Cyclone is a wooden roller coaster located at Luna Park in Coney Island, Brooklyn, New York.

In spring 1972, it was announced that Cyclone would be removed to make way for expansion of a nearby aquarium. Following the Save The Cyclone campaign, this did not happen and the coaster reopened on July 3, 1975.

The ride was declared a city landmark in 1988 and a national landmark in 1991. The Brooklyn Cyclones baseball team is named after the Cyclone.

The ride was awarded ACE Roller Coaster Landmark status by American Coaster Enthusiasts on June 29, 2002.

The ride underwent refurbishment work by Great Coasters International and Skyline Attractions in the late 2010s.

The Cyclone's compact layout has been duplicated on many other roller coasters.

The Cyclone does not use a ride computer. Braking ability is provided by manually operated skid brakes. The ride mechanics are mostly unchanged from the ride's opening.

The ride is only designed to run one train at a time.


 * On May 26, 1985, a 29-year-old male stood up and was killed after striking his head on a part of the structure.
 * On August 23, 1988, a maintenance worker was killed after assuming an unauthorized riding position and being ejected from the car. He fell 30 feet onto another section of track.
 * On July 31, 2007, a 53-year-old man suffered several crushed vertebrae in his neck while on the ride. He died to to complications in surgery 4 days later.
 * A woman claimed to be seriously injured after riding the Cyclone in 2008. Despite being partially at fault for the incident, she was awarded $1.5 million in damages.
